description:
Each player has a snake which is made of colorful pearls. The game has no board, instead, a starting line and a finish line are determined at the players' discretion. The snakes are laid out at the starting line.

Each player rolls two color dice in his/her turn, then moves one end of his/her snake forward until the two colors are adjacent to each other. The first snake to reach the finish line is the winner.

The game is luck-driven, but there is minimal decision making for the kids, as they have to understand which end they should move forward in order to go faster.

(User summary by HilkMAN)

GAME OVERVIEW 
Players are Luminaries -- a royal order of star cartographers, mapping out the night sky. Over the course of the game, players will take turns chaining stars together as they sail to the moon! 
 GAMEPLAY 
Players race across the map trying to chain stars together and to score points by arranging sets of stars into constellations. 
The player who scores the most points over the course of the game is the winner!

Each player has a snail which consists of a shell and a long body with colored rings around it. A starting and finishing line is laid out at the players' discretion.

The players then take turns rolling a color die and moving the shell of their snail forward until the ring in the corresponding color can be seen behind the body. Afterwards, they hold the shell in place and pull the body back into the shell. The first player to reach the finish line this way is the winner.

The game has no resemblance with Reinhold Wittig's earlier game of the same name (published by Edition Perlhuhn).

(User summary by HilkMAN)

While the game looks like a simple roll and move game for the very young at first is actually more than that, as players have a few options. For instance, a roll can be re-rolled, but the original roll has to be given away to another player. The die has symbols instead of colors and numbers. The goal is to reach the sun.

The game was published in two versions, one regular sized, one giant one (150 cm game board blanket, "meeples" up to 16 cm high).

(User summary by HilkMAN)

From the website www.flipitpapercombat.com

Flipit: Paper Combat is a fast-paced tabletop miniatures game in a setting similar to WWII. Players lead forces from one of the four major nations into the conflict for Jurop. Armies are built of models players can download, print and build for almost free (you still have to supply the ink, paper, and glue). There are simple starter rules available, which may be supplimented by optional advanced rules to make gameplay more challenging.

An expansion/scenario pack for Advanced Squad Leader. Like the previous scenario sets produced by the Kansas City Irregular ASL Club for their annual March Madness tournament in Kansas City, Kansas, this set is limited to 75 numbered copies.

This module focuses in on 6 WWII-era battles and, uniquely, one Cold War action. Scenarios cover:

•Usi-Usa, Siberia 1942 (gulag escapees take up arms against Soviets)
•Ivaskovic, Ukraine 1942 (anti-partisan actions by Slovak troops)
•Warsaw, Poland 1943 (Jewish uprising in Warsaw Ghetto)
•Paris 1944 (French underground fighters occupy Grand Palais)
•Rembertow, Poland 1945 (Polish partisans assault NKVD holding camp)
•Shaba, Zaire 1978 (French paratroopers respond to Angolan aggression)

Contents:
•7 scenarios
•2 overlays

GAME OVERVIEW
The mythic age is coming to an end with each of the world's races vying to push each other into legend. Each race’s strongest warband has risen to meet the call of the new world. Edda puts you in contol of a warband, granting you abilities and strengths to lead your brothers.

* Stormbringers, of the Vanir, from the frozen seas of the North, fierce and cold-blooded.
    * Banehammers, of the Light Elves, closest to the World Tree, radiant and aloof with old ways and rivalries.
    * Cursewalkers, of the Jotun, lurking the darklands of the giants, plotting and terrible with the hunger of men.
    * Ironcrushers, of the Aesir, from the wilds of Asgard, conquest-bent, trampling everyone underfoot.
    * Stonebiters, of the Dark Elves, from the rotting roots of the World Tree, cunning and vengeful against brethren.

Yet, as you and your warband seek conquest over land and seas, you must vie not only against each other, but also against the curse of the Runes -- gifts of great power and destruction! Will your warband survive into the next age?

GAMEPLAY
Players explore the map trying to claim lands and to score points by building, settling, and raiding. The player who scores the most points over the course of the game is the winner!

When the world of Summoner Master is played as Chess equal to this game.

After setup the modular board, each player will lay down units like the Chess but different from Chess, each unit has its own movement pattern, attack range, HP and can be upgradable to be more powerful unit in the game.

The example of units in the game are Lord, Dragon, Priest, Clown, Assassin, Soldier, etc.

The dice is involved in the game for resolving the effectiveness of each unit ability such as Avoid, Reflect or Defend.

There are 2 goals of the game. First is capturing the opponent's Lord unit (by reducing its HP to 0 or less) as the eliminating of the King in Chess OR capturing as many opponent's units that can reach the unit level summation criteria. (total of 15 levels or more in all variances of the first edition)

The colorful Princess Hide & Seek Game is a memory and matching game for children ages 18 months and up. It was published by Cardinal Industries with a Disney Princess theme.

Children match easy-to-handle pictures in large plastic frames with magical Disney scenes on four separate game boards. One board represents a tea party in a garden, another represents a scene in a room full of toys, a third represents a fancy dress ball, and the final board is a playground scene. The faces are popular Disney characters.

This game can be played solitaire or in a group with each child using an indivdual game board.

Includes 4 boards, 20 tokens, and rules.

Cardinal Item # 57714

High Fantasy Theme. Each player represents the head of a Clan of Dwarves that collectively form a single stronghold in the center of a Mountain infested with Monster Lairs. Players choose each turn the role of one of 8 leaders to give them special abilities and benefits during the turn. Players dig, fight, mine, and build rooms.

The Complete time span of Human Civilization is the subject of this card game for 2-6 players. Players pick cards from 4 successive decks (Ancient, Medieval, Revolution, and Modern)  to increase the value of their civilization in 5 categories: Culture, Military, Vitality, Resources, and Stability. Pick the Leaders, Wonders, and Advances you need. Change your form of Government. Win Wars to steal Victory from your opponent.

Historical Game for 2-5 players. Each player controls 1 of the following Italian powers during the 15th century Renaissance:  Republic of Florence, Republic of Venice, Duchy of Milan, Kingdom of Naples, and Papal States. Players compete to improve their scores in 6 Categories: Religion, Politics, Humanities, Military, Buildings, and Economics. Having the lead in a category gives you a special ability as long as you are able to maintain that lead.

Bidding Game for 3-5 players. Theme: Imperial Expansion of European Powers circa 1600-1750. Players compete for control in 10 Global Regions by assigning influence cards of differing values. Control of a region awards victory points. Regions gradually grow in value (in a partially player-determined fashion) as the game progresses. Finally, Events at the end of each turn can radically change influence totals in each of the Regions.

Board Game for 3-6 players. Theme: Rise and fall of various Native Empires in Western and Sub-Saharan Africa circa 1100-1700 AD. Empires come and go. Each empire is described by ratings in Government, Religion, and Economy. Players simultaneously build up, vie for control, and cause the downfall of multiple Empires.
This is an educational game that came bundled with a book called "Involvement in Biology Today".  Players move around the board simulating the body's metabolism.

The Elevation of Privilege Card Game is a game released by Microsoft which models threat modelling in software development.

Elevation of Privilege is the easy way to get started threat modeling. Threat modeling is a core component of the design phase in the Microsoft Security Development Lifecycle (SDL).

The Elevation of Privilege (EoP) card game helps clarify the details of threat modeling and examines possible threats to software and computer systems.

The EoP game focuses on the following threats:

* Spoofing
    * Tampering
    * Repudiation
    * Information Disclosure
    * Denial of Service
    * Elevation of Privilege

EoP uses a simple point system that allows you to challenge other developers and become your opponent’s biggest threat.

This is basically a board game version of Charades, but it does outlaw spelling out words.  You break into two teams, and use basic charades style play to act out the card clues.  The time or round limit is set for the game by consensus. There's an electronic timer, and a briefing on basic mime signs.  There's an electronic timer, and a die, along with the box of cards with 3 subjects each on them.  The die is used prior to drawing a card of subjects.  Roll a 6, and you get to pick any of the five subjects.  Score is based on having the shortest times - recorded times are totaled. Penalties are assessed for using illegal means of communication, adding 30 seconds (points) for each violation.
This is a game that appears to utilize questions to test your knowledge of etiquette.  Published in 1988 by Mountain Top games.

This is a two player game intended to teach the players about how molecules are built from atoms. Each player receives a Star-Castle Component Card (a list of four molecules) at the beginning of the game and draw Character (Element) cards and special action cards to build the molecule formulas. The first player to build three of the four molecules on his/her card wins. Or as it says on the rule sheet "When you win- stand up and say "I ESCAPED FROM SUPERNOVA!"

The players must decide quickly. As soon as a player turns over a card (s)he calls "Flip!" which means that the picture is the same on both sides of the card. (S)he says "Flop!" if (s)he is sure that the upturned picture is different. The player with the most different cards at the end wins the game!

Contents:
• 20 wooden cards with pictures printed on both sides
• 1 coloured Beleduc Box

This is a question and answer game with 6 large cards and 136 small ones. The large cards are filled in when a question on the small cards is answered correctly.

Published in England.

A promotional game with a somewhat contrived theme. A friend of the players' has been locked up in a seedy warehouse for unclear reasons. The players race across town using various means of transportation. Before reaching the warehouse each player must also acquire a cell phone with a Telia subscription (guess none of them thought that was worth having for any other reason) so they can receive an access code (from whom?). Once the friend has been sprung from captivity he must be brought back home, and during this stage players try to "steal" him from each other (can't they all just get along?).

Question game about the trilogy "Les Bronzés"

We play a character of "Les Bronzés" (Gigi, Bernard, Nathalie, Jean-Claude, Jérôme or Popeye) and we need to join The Prunus first after start at Ganaswinda and a little break in a ski station.

On each turn we roll a dice and answer a question until we miss an answer.

Question are about :
. Les Bronzés
. Les Bronzés font du ski
. Les Bronzés, amis pour la vie
. Knowledges about scenes or quotes
. About Les Bronzés
A simple, portable trivia game from Kärnan's range of Pocket Geni variants. 450 questions.

From the rulebook:

Each player walks his counters across the board. If he lands on a space marked with one of the seven different plant symbols, he may take the matching plant card. But before all 64 board spaces have been "planted" with altogether four kinds of plant, the amateur gardener may suffer several cruel turns of fortune. Drought may wither his lawn, sunflowers or plums, caterpillars and greenfly attack his pansies and lettuce beds. The lucky player will be protected against such ravages by having earlier drawn an "antidote" card, such as insurance against flooding, bug killer to combat a plague of maybugs. A player landing on the "Garden Party" space is served a small refreshment for relaxation, such as a sweet or a glass of lemonade. Adults will prefer a glass of the harder stuff.
Winner is the first player to plant the 16 spaces of his garden with 4 different types of plant cards.

Fairly abstract game supplied with a naval theme. Players try to cross the board with their ship while firing mines to block the progress of enemy ships. The mines and ships all have pegs which fit in the grid of holes on the board. There are a few different ways of playing depending on the number of players and desired playing time.

Draw a card and count your way towards home. You're at the home gate and nothing's gonna stop you now. Nothing that is, except the card your opponent just drew...STING!. You're back where you started from. That's STING, the fast paced game of cards and marbles that combines a healthy dose of strategy and a little bit of luck to create a board game like nothing you've ever played before.

A Swedish boxing game from the late 50s or early 60s, probably occasioned by the international success of Swedish boxer Ingemar "Ingo" Johansson. You play out a schedule of 12 prizefights. If no winner has been established after this, a round of finals is played between the two leading boxers. There is also an economical element that includes betting. The fights themselves are resolved using offensive and defensive cards with labels like "Jab" or "Uppercut" and accompanying point values. Some cards lead to the drawing of chance cards that affect the course of the fight. It is possible to win matches by knockout or points.

Players move their pieces across an abstract grid, with lines of varying orientation and/or curvature representing different forms of transportation. The goal is to reach Rome in the centre of the board. Movement is executed using randomly dealt cards which empower players to move a certain number of steps or using a certain means of transportation.

The game contains a tool box, six chips which show two different tools on their two sides (each tool appears twice) and 16 repair cards which show things that have to be repaired and the three tools needed for that.

One card is flipped open and all chips are put into the tool box. The active player shakes the tool box well, then flips it over and checks whether the chips (which fell out) show the three necessary tool symbols. If yes, then the player gets the card. If not, the chips are returned to the tool box and the box is passed on to the next player. The player with most cards at the end of the game is the winner.

From the card box:

Smile Squad is a two-player card game for kids ages 6-11.  Players compete to be the first to score 32 points or more - the number of teeth in a healthy grown-up smile - while learning important dental health information.  By matching cards, players command heroes to perform actions that defeat villains.

Simple roll and move game based on characters from a Swedish series of popular children's books written by Gösta Knutsson. You move across the city of Uppsala (where the books are set) and get the occasional typical "lose your next turn" or "move four spaces ahead" events, accompanied by short descriptions of cat antics. The box notes the game was made with permission from Knutsson's widow Erna, and it's not impossible she's had some input on the game's design.

Surround * Capture * Conquer

miQube Duo takes the award winning miQube to the next level. Players gain points by surrounding, capturing and conquering territory on the board’s surface by strategically selecting and placing games pieces so that they block and cover their opponents. Its durable wooden design will ensure it can withstand the rigours of play.

* Anyone from the age of 7 upwards can enjoy this game!
    * Simple rules for up to 2-4 players
    * The perfect game for family and friends
    * Encourages planning ahead to outsmart your opponent
    * Requiring all the strategic cunning of a chess game, but with a three dimensional twist

Small trivia game about literature; with 100 questions and answers. The game itself is like a notebook (with spiral binding). Each page contains 2 questions and 4 nswers, the back side contains the correct answers with additional information.

The graphic design is very similar to Huch & Friends' larger "Welt der Bücher".

     Two new roles: The Revolutionary and the Zombie Master.

boardgame: New Plants (fan expansion for Power Grid)
description:
(from rules)
This expansion contains eight new plants and assorted other buildings for Power Grid. It was designed by Mike
Young, using some art created by Chris Jones (Goldenturkey from Board Game Geek).

From the publisher:

idQuest: A new game of lies and laughter. Imagine showing up at a party with some sophisticated polygraph equipment. Then imagine what it would be like to strap it on some unsuspecting soul.
Now let's imagine putting that person through a series of all sorts of embarrassing questions. Can't you just see all the other guests laughing every time the machine registers a lie? Imagine the fun, imagine the spectacle...Imagine no more!

Call it the perfect ice breaker, a novelty, a gizmo from hell, call it idQuest the lie detector game.

idQuest is a socially interactive adult party game that consists of a hand held lie detector and 216 socially provocative questions. The lie detector was developed and patented by a police polygraph examiner and an electronics engineer. The questions are designed to contrast the id and the ego. The questions are about things that most people have done or thought about, but don't want to admit.

Players are divided up into two teams. After determining who will be questioned first, the question cards are then randomly selected and read to all players.

The other team must then discuss which question will generate the highest emotional response from the player being questioned. Once this has been decided, with the lie detector engaged, the two questions are asked. The hand held lie detector will then display the size of the emotional response to each question using an illuminated bar graph. If the opposing team guessed correctly the device sounds an incriminating alarm and they are awarded a point.

You'll laugh at each other! You'll laugh at yourself! You'll find out how much you don't know about friends, and how much we all have in common.

Nominated by the National Research Council as one of the most innovative projects of 1996.

The is the first published edition of the Lords of the Sierra Madre game, which was later re-designed and re-published by Decision Games in 1995.

Players are hacendados, rich foreigners who are looking to multiply their fortunes in the rich and newly open for business land in Northern Mexico, under the rule of the pro-business dictator Porfirio Diaz.

The game is divided into strategic and tactical rounds.  During strategic rounds (representing one quarter of a year) the hacendados bid on investments, construct enterprises, bribe troops, plot revolutions, and spy on each other.  During tactical rounds (each a single day) the players move their troops, indians, bandidos, railguns and policia around the map to harass (tax, kidnap, arrest, burn) one another and to protect their own enterprises.

The hex map covers a large swath of northern mexico and the southwest US... ranging from El Fuerte Sinaloa in the South, to Florence, Arizona to the North, to Yuma, Arizona in the West and El Paso, Texas in the East.  Each hex is 10 miles.  Players moving units in tactical rounds must contend with all of the hazards the wild west has to offer via an event table.

Semi-cooperative play is often rewarded, as there are no rules regarding transactions and deals that can be made, and many enterprises are highly dangerous and the risk is often best distributed among multiple players.  There can, however, only be one winner... and deals are only as good as your ability to enforce them with strength of arms.  Welcome to Mexico.

Players can run for office, lead revolutions, or become bandit leaders, but the hacendado with the most gold (ill-gotten or otherwise) at the end of the game wins.

Re-implemented by:

    Lords of the Sierra Madre (Second Edition)

boardgame: Star Blazers Technical Manual and Warship Recognition Guide
description:
From the Author's Preface:

This manual provides players of the Star Blazers Fleet Battle System with detailed explanations of the warships and battle craft used in the game. This is also the first English language reference work to provide a comprehensive catalog and history of the spacecraft and weapon systems portrayed in the television and movie series.

The hardware included in this manual is drawn from the first two Star Blazers television series and their associated movie releases. The statistical data was translated with permission from the publications of Academy Press, a company owned by Yoshinobu Nishizaki of Voyager Entertainment, Japan.

From the Back of the Book:

At last, the definitive reference book for Star Blazers fans! This data book is the first ever to bring together the ships and weapons of all three space powers from the acclaimed science fiction animated series.

Over 490 illustrations depict the mechanical exterior views of ships of the Earth Defense Force, the Gamilon Empire, and the White Comet Empire, as well as battle craft, support craft, weapons systems, and battle maps.

Fleet organization, command structure, weapons systems, and tactical doctrines of each power are covered in extensive detail. Mission profiles of all ships and battle craft describe the purpose and role each vessel serves in its respective fleet.

In this promotional board game by Swedish insurance company Skandia, players move around a Monopoly-style board, drawing various types of event cards. They will get the chance to invest in property like cars, houses or diamonds, hoping to sell them at a profit or else convert them to cash at the end of the game. They may also get to purchase tickets in an intermittently triggered lottery (because winning the lottery and speculating in diamonds are the two surest ways of getting rich, or something).

Everything and anything in the game is in danger of damage or accidents, although having the right insurance helps with this. Several of the risk vs. reward elements of the game are easily solvable and serve to encourage behaviour that is illegal or economically irrational in reality, like casino gambling or diamond smuggling, which seems a little odd considering the theme. The game is played for an arbitrary amount of time; the rules suggest playing until a point in time agreed upon in advance is reached, after which five more moves are played out. The player with the most money is then declared the winner.

Another game based on a local implementation of the American Idol TV show concept, specifically the Swedish one. Consequently it involves trivia, singing and other elements that will cause non-fans of the franchise to want to run away quickly. Appears to be most closely related to American Idol All-Star Challenge DVD Game, although this one has 2 DVDs. Apparently expands on a previous game called Idol Karaoke.

Panzertruppen 1990 is a 285th scale modern miniatures wargame that covers the world's modern armies, circa 1990.

From the Introduction
War is an interplay between all the assets of one side against those of the other. In Panzertruppen 1990 we have attempted to bring together all of the aspects of the modern battlefield. The engagements will vary from the platoon and company level to larger battalion and regimental size units incorporating all supporting elements such as mortars, artillery, strike aircraft and relying on sophisticated radars, lasers, computers and communications equipment.

Panzertuppen 1990 has for the first time combined both the small and large unit encounter into the most playable  modern combat rules on the market today. These rules reflect the extreme speed of movement and attribution of the modern battlefield environment.

From the Introduction
This book is for use with Legionnaire, Interceptor, Leviathan, and Centurion. It provides background information for all the inhabited worlds in Shannedam County, as well as information on the military forces in the County for use  with these games.

User Addendum
Along with the various sections on system information, this supplement also introduces various types of Orbital Industrial Facilities, Ground Legion Bases, & Naval Bases.

A horse-racing game.

No dice, no skill!  An unusual mechanism. Each horse moves by having its colour indicated by an arrow on the base of the last horse to move, on a variable track with the colours in varied sequence. Completely predetermined as soon as the track is laid and the first horse is chosen, but completely unpredictable.

Re-implementation of:
Alternardo (that game used the same basic mechanism, but with a fixed track of six lanes)

DAK '44 is a supplement for the Panzer Grenadier system based on the potential battles of the continuation of action in the western desert after Alamein. This module is not playable by itself, but requires ownership of our Afrika Korps, Beyond Normandy, Cassino '44 and Desert Rats games. There are 10 scenarios included.

There are 88 new German pieces in the desert scheme first introduced in Afrika Korps. They include Tiger, Tiger II and Panther tanks, Hetzer tank destroyers, Wespe self-propelled artillery plus the anti-tank guns, mortars and rocket launchers of Germany's 1944 arsenal.

DAK '44 is sold BOTH as a .pdf download and as a printed booklet with actual playing pieces.

description:
Dice Towers is a two player game where each player uses over 30 dice to build prestigious towers, mine for gold and battle head to head with their opponent.

The game lasts 10 rounds with scoring after round 5 and round 10.  Each round, both players roll 3 or more dice and typically use their dice one at a time to increase the height of a tower, gather the next available gold, fortify their troops on the battlefield or carry over to the next round.

Dice Towers was designed and created for the Dicefest contest - a tribute to Chessex run by Kai Bettzieche.

description:

description:
From the publisher of Satori: the Radical Forgiveness Board Game. . .

Satori is a really fun way to let go of emotional baggage — forgiving yourself and others in the process — easily and quickly. It promotes laughter, enlightenment, insight and growth — both spiritual and personal.

Quite amazingly, 'Satori' gives us the exact insights we need to move beyond vicitimhood and to heal our lives. The game mirrors for us our own wisdom in having created the very circumstances of our own healing. It leads us to where we can reframe the past and see the perfection in it. It can be prophetic in showing us issues we didn’t even know we had. And It connects us to the truth of who we are. It’s quite a game!

OBJECTIVE

The goal is to proceed through four levels of consciousness in order to get to Satori — total awareness.

EVENT AND CONTEXT CARDS

Each player picks an Event card and a Context card. (9 times out of 10 these are a direct hit on an issue the person needs to work on). Each player in turn creates a victim story around that situation and places his/her playing piece in Victimland to start the game.

THE LANDING SPACES

Of course the journey is one fraught with difficulties and repleat with blessings, depending upon which space you land.  The landing spaces will direct you to look at your beliefs, blocks, karma, stories and projections etc., with instructions or by drawing cards.

NEW STORY CARDS

These help you shift out of your victim story - with all its negative beliefs and energy blocks, and move into a new story.

'SATORI'

No matter who gets there first, we want for everyone else to come to Satori. At that point, each player gets a chance to reframe their story and to read an affirmation.

THE RESULTS

People report feeling lighter, feeling free from pain or the weight of guilt and anger, more compassionate and more loving.

description:
A lavishly produced game which tries to be a straight simulation of playing all 18 holes on the course at St Andrews, the home of Golf. The game has 9 huge colour printed, double-sided boards, laying out an accurate depiction of the course. Players shots are randomise by using a spinner and cards for direction and distance. There are a series of long clear plastic rules which are your clubs. You choose which one to use as you progress. These mark the point where your ball finally lands, based upon the randomisation.

The rules are in English, French, German, Italian and Dutch. There is no designer given, nor actual year of production, but the score-pads included refer to St Andrews as the home of the 1978 Open, suggesting it was produced around then.

In the series of quiz games from DIDACTO, we, of course, had to include science. The modern era has been marked by scientific discoveries, and we must understand its main components.

This game will let you penetrate the world of geniuses and their inventions. Once immersed, not in Archimedes' bathtub, but in a fascinating universe in which you might perhaps be brought to exclaim "Eureka!"

(Translation from the instruction package)

"Eurêka!" is a game in which multiple players compete in trying to complete more of the 13 numbered sets of 4 cards than any other player. Every card contains several (around 5-10) trivia questions. After having distributed the cards evenly among players, gameplay starts. On his turn, a player will ask for a particular card of a set of which he has at least one other card. Its owner will ask the first question on the card - should that card ever be asked again, the second question will be asked, and so forth. Whenever a player answers correctly, the card is transferred to him; whenever a player completes a series or wrongfully answers a question, it becomes the next player in clockwise order's turn. The game ends either after all sets are completed, or after a set time deadline, to the players' choice.

Questions vary in difficulty, as some of them can easily be answered by any 6th grader while some others require an acute knowledge of particular disciplines. Mathematics, physics, chemistry, engineering, computer science and biology are all disciplines that this game addresses.

(Description by BGG user jgag)

A series of three expansion books for Warhammer 40,000 third edition.  Like the Book of the Astronomican for Rogue Trader, this short-lived annual collected articles from White Dwarf featuring various new rules, updates to the game, and army lists - some official, others merely experimental.  There is substantial overlap between the volumes as each subsequent one was in part meant to update and/or collect much of the material of the previous.
  
The original in the series, Chapter Approved, was published in 2001, with Chapter Approved 2003 following in 2002 and Chapter Approved 2004 in 2003 (the year in the title indicating the coming year rather than when it was published).  They were also known as the Second, Third, and Fourth Book of the Astronomican, respectively, in homage to the original from Rogue Trader.

Printed in the US, each game of Hockey Canada RPS 27 includes 18 high-quality puzzle pieces and there are 27 pieces to collect in all.

The premiere edition of RPS 27 features classic Team Canada hockey jerseys from 1920 to 2010.

Tank Traders is an auction game in which you build terminals with building cards you acquire through 3 kinds of auctions: ascending-price, descending price and sealed-price. The player who manages to build 2 terminals first, wins the game.

From the back of the box: An exciting and fast family game in which strategy, bluff and commercial spirit come together. Conquer the world of logistics by building your own independent tank terminal network.

Seppuku is a form of Japanese ritual suicide by disembowelment (“cutting the belly”). This practice was a key part of the Bushido, the code of the samurai warriors, and was used to avoid falling into enemy hands, and to attenuate shame. One might say that death is the very center of the Bushido, as the overall purpose- to die well and with one's honor intact.

You are a Daimyo, a powerful feudal ruler in ancient Japan. You must hire samurai to protect your land, and win battles courageously. The first Daimyo whose clan obtains the “Seven Virtues” of the Bushido will be announced as the winner.

What's new in the 2nd Edition? The major revision is the replacement of koku (rice) cards with koku chits. Koku can be added to your inventory each turn which dramatically speeds up game play, allows for quicker seeding of samurai and creates bigger, more exciting battles. Additionally Seppuku takes advantage of The Game Crafter's custom dice and packaging giving the game a much more professional look. Enjoy!
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Nya Sverigespelet is a simple Swedish trivia game where players move around a map of Sweden and answer questions afterwards. This game is a modernisation of an older Sverigespelet (without the "Nya" ("New")), also by Eric Bojs.

Please note that this is not the same game as Dan Glimne's Sverigespelet from the same publisher. The name was re-cycled!

From the back of the box:
A delightful adding and subtracting game.

Kids will be so fascinated by the colorful insects, they won't realize they're learning math. Players race around the charming nature path landing on "plus" and "minus" stepping-stones along the way.

They'll count colorful bugs as they put them in their jars. Bugs stick magnetically to the jars' surfaces. But, watch out! Land on a "minus" and some bugs escape from your collection.

The winner is the player with the most bugs in his jar when the first player reaches the end of the path.

Contents:

     1 game board (14" x 14" divided into large puzzle pieces)
     4 bug collecting jars - magnetic receptive (4" x 6")
     42 exciting magnetic bugs (approx. 1 1/2")
     4 player pieces
     1 game die
     instructions
     handy storage tote
     2 to 4 players


Education aspects:

     Adding and subtracting
     No reading required
     Experiencing magnetics
     Turn taking
     Bug knowledge
     Independent learning

The game where you win if you tag along the longest, but do the least amount of work.

"Vem vill bli byråkrat?" (Who wants to be a bureaucrat?) is a fairly simple card game. Your goal is to get rid of cards by collecting sets. The game itself is quite humorous, since all cards are actual excuses (as to why a certain case seems to have stranded, for example) from real bureaucrats (and others) in Sweden.

Players will also coöperate slightly; a player can "join in" when someone else gets rid of a case. But the goal in the game - as well as in real life bureaucracy - is to get rid of all the cases, without actually doing any amount of work towards them.

This is actually five games in one using 60 blocks that resemble Jenga blocks.  Each is a building game of sorts.

Totropolous: This is fairly Jenga-like, take turns removing a block from the tower of 60 blocks, placing the removed block on the top of teh tower.

Staircase: starting with one block placed flatwise, take turns building a tower with each next block twisted slightly on its axis.

Superstack: Form a stack four layers tall, each containing four blocks.  You oscillate between building stacks as in Tortopolous and 7 stairs as in staircase.

23: You alternate in turns building the Roman numeral 2 then the Roman numeral 3 on top of it, then 2 again on top of the 3, etc.

Expert Builder: This is identical to Tortopolous, except, you are restricted in removing blocks from teh original structure and not anywhere in the tower.

All of these games continue until the towers fall over.

Run! Dodge! Catch! Dodge! Throw! Dodge! Win!


"There are many variations of the game, but generally the main objective of each team is to eliminate all members of the opposing team by hitting them with thrown balls, [or] catching a ball thrown by a member of the opposing team"

Hello Coach! Welcome to the furiously competitive sport of Checkerboard Dodgeball! Prepare for a fast game of team elimination!

In games of Checkerboard Dodgeball!, Coaches draft teams of 5 players to face off on a standard checker or chessboard. Each round, Coaches select up to three players with unique abilities and activation speeds to run and throw balls at opposing players. Hits are determined by opposing die rolls - often, the higher roll hitting and knocking a player out of the game. A defender's lucky roll dodges or even catches the ball, taking the thrower out and bringing a friendly player back into play!

Coaches mitigate luck with smart use of Momentum, Extra Effort! tokens and unique Player abilities.

The Coach that puts out all the other Coach’s Players wins!

Playing time is normally under 20 minutes.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Inspired by games from Crokinole to Catacombs to Taktika, Conflickt: Field of Slaughter is a two to six player dexterity game of strategic and tactical combat with a fantasy war theme.  Asymmetric in design, each player leads a faction of warriors (melee, ranged, and special units) with unique colors and properties in both team and free-for-all battles to rout the other armies.

Players of Conflickt can command the Humans (yellow), Goblins (green), Elves (blue), Orcs (red), Dwarves (orange), or Undead (purple).  Each option plays differently, with varying numbers of warriors and unique special units employing awesome powers that can completely change the game.

Any relatively smooth table can serve as the eponymous Field of Slaughter.  Strike quickly and strike true, or death awaits from an enemy with a better plan!

Commissioned by a museum, some art collectors come together to organize an exhibition in honor of 2 grandmasters.  The art collectors have 2 goals:
- make sure that their own paintings are exhibited,
- make sure that their own visitors stand with the most important paintings.

Setup of the game: depending on how many players, each player receives a certain amount of visitors (tokens) and a certain number of paintings.  The paintings from each player are put together in a certain way in the middle of the table (in a circle, triangle or square).

Each round has 4 phases:
- phase 1: putting in or move your visitors to a painting or let them take a break
- phase 2: a painting disappears from the table (by throwing a dice)
- phase 3: move the guide (the painting which is visited by the guide can't be removed in phase 2)
- phase 4: new start player

The game ends when only 2 painters are left.  The winner is the player with the most points.
You earn point for:
- each own painting that is left on the table (1 point)
- each own visitor on a painting (the number of points mentioned on the painting)

(description taken from the rulebook)

Weltmissionsfahrt is a roll-and-move game published by the "Schweizerische Missionsgesellschaft Bethlehem" some time between 1921 and 1938. The players have to start their journey in Rome. They move two pawns representing missionaries across a world map. The winner is the player whose two pawns return to Rome the fastest.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
A game of claustrophobic terror and survival in a hostile environment.

In an underground military base, experiments are done in order to create a new biological weapon. Mutations of dangerous infectious agents and other threats are kept there.
Suddenly an accident occurs in a laboratory, releasing a lethal virus all over the place. Some of the first victims already suffer the effects of the new disease, and rise up from the dead, returning to life as zombies hungry for human flesh.
Some survivors still resist the virus, but their time is short. At any time, they can become a feast for the undead, or succumb to the disease and increase the horde of brain-eaters.

The players are in the role of some of these survivors, and should explore the site facing the zombies, find the experimental cure and escape.

Prepare, load your weapons and run! There is only one way out.

A summary of the gameplay:

The characters start at the central sector of the board, that is already revealed. The goal is to find the antidote and escape through the hatch in the center of the board. To do this, the players must spend action points to do actions like walk, fight and search.
When a character enters in a unrevealed room, he must take one tile from the pile, and place it adjacent to the room he is coming from, connecting the doors. Then he rolls the encounter dice, that shows if he met zombies, items or nothing.
The fight is conducted by the range and the requirements that each weapon has to kill or hurt the zombies. Then the player rolls a dice and see if the result satisfies those requirements. He can spend action points to add to the result.
The items are managed in the inventory, that shows how many items the character can carry.
When the player choose to search, he takes a card from the correspondent sector pile. It can be items, good or bad events, weapons or the antidote.
When a player finishes his move, then the next player plays with the zombies on the board. He can move and attack with all zombies.
After that, the same player now plays with his human character, an it goes on. When the round is complete, move the players' infection marker. If someone passes the last
mark, he becomes a zombie.
There are some details in the rules about spending action points, fighting, events and items.
The first player to find the cure and get back to the hatch is the winner.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: On Guard: A Military Table Game
description:
A simple point to point movement game for 2 to 4 players.

User Summary of the rules:  One player acts as the Fort Guard and walks a specified route based on a spinner.  The other player(s) are the enemy and must try to get by the Guard, reconnoiter the fort and return to the start.  If the Guard catches a player they must then be "On Guard".

Movement is controlled by a spinner (1 to 4).

The Marvel Power Game is a pull-out board game with multiple win conditions.

Gameplay consists of moving your character around the game board, accumulating cards, and battling your opponent by comparing the value of your cards.

Win the game by being the first player to accomplish one of the following conditions:

(A) Accumulate 200 Power Points.
(B) Reduce your opponent to 0 Power Points
(C) Land on your opponent's space.

The Getta Goff Challenge games are a series of milk cap games (also known as Pog) with special rules and instructions meant to add a more educational twist to standard Pog.

Traditional Pog is played by stacking a variety of milk caps (aka Pogs) on top of a playing mat. Players then take turns throwing a "Slammer" (which is usually a much thicker plastic or metal Pog) onto the top of the stack in an attempt flip as many of the stacked Pogs upside down as possible. Generally, players then take  ownership of any Pogs that flipped.

In Advanced Math Getta Goff, for example, the players must carry out mathematical operations based on the instructions on the flipped Pogs.

This is a fast-paced word game from the 1960s.

Play is based upon card/letter combinations. A plastic spinner is used to randomly generate a letter of the alphabet. A card randomly drawn from the deck gives the players a "category" and the first contestant to blurt out an answer wins the round. The winner is awarded that card and the player with the most cards at the end of the game is the champion.

Example of play: The letter is a "T". The card reads "A part of a car." so the first geek to shout "Tire!" or "Transmission!" or whatever wins that round.

Includes 48 cards, the spinner, a score pad, and rules.

Eldon Item #885

boardgame: Tras el Unicornio: Searching the Unicorn
description:
According to a legend in 1471 by order of Henry IV King of Castille, called the Impotent, set out one expedition to Africa, beyond the land of the Moors.
The secret aim of this expedition was to hunt a unicorn, since it was assumed that the horn of this mythological creature could increase the virility of the king.
In 1946 the results of the exhumation of the corpse of Henry IV  alongside the king's mummified remains found a gray spindle object, which was proved to be a fragment of African rhino horn.

So begins and ends the novel "In Search of the Unicorn" by Juan Eslava Galan awarderd Planeta Prize in 1987, being the original idea for this game. Each player leads an expedition sent to hunt the unicorn, but in the way they could get other valuable things like gold, trading on the Silk Road and to evangelize people in the name of his master Henry IV of Castille. All this if the expeditioners survived the attacks of nomade tribes with whom they have to fight. Many dangers and soldiers will die during the way and what is worse, maybe you the leader will also fall before reaching your goal ... who will get the horn and gets the grace of the king?

---

En 1471 por orden de Enrique IV de Castilla, llamado el Impotente, parte una expedición desde Segovia con destino los pastizales de Africa, más allá de la tierra de los moros.
El objetivo secreto de esta expedición era cazar un unicornio, ya que se suponía que su cuerno podía aumentar la virilidad del rey.
Según reza el Acta de la Exhumación del cadáver de Enrique IV de 1946, junto a los restos momificados del rey se encontró un objeto fusiforme gris, que una vez identificado por el C.S.I.C. resultó ser un fragmento de cuerno de rinoceronte africano.

Así comienza y termina la novela “En busca del unicornio” de Juan Eslava Galán  que fue Premio Planeta en 1987, y  que sirve como idea para este juego. Cada jugador dirige una expedición para cazar el unicornio, pero por el camino podrá conseguir oro, comerciar en la ruta de la seda y hasta evangelizar pueblos para ensalzar la figura de su señor Enrique IV de Castilla. Todo esto si sobrevive a los ataques de los pueblos nómadas con los que tendrá que luchar. A lo largo de la expedición es posible que tus compañeros pierdan la vida y lo que es peor, a lo mejor tu también caes…¿serás tu quien gane el favor del rey?

Mad Bishops is a two-player game which uses a 10x10 checkerboard, 25 red checkers and 25 blue checkers.  The board is initially set up as shown in the figure.

The checkers function like Chess bishops with two restrictions.  On your turn you must either kill, or move into position to kill.  If a checker is already in position to kill, it can only be used to kill. Killing is not mandatory.  I.e. if you can move an unengaged checker to engage the enemy, you can do that instead.

Mad Bishops is a game of annihilation.  I.e., you must kill all enemy checkers to win.

From the box:

"Bid$ is a truly challenging experience that is totally unique from most board games in that any property is available, if you are willing to bid the right price.  You can taste the satisfaction of forcing your opponents to pay too much for their modest properties, or jump at the opportunity of stealing a legitimate good deal by being shrewd and cunning.  If you like fast paced competitive fun that keeps the chance factor to a minimum, try Bid$, the game where you do control your own destiny."

Object of the game (2-6 players) - To make as much money as possible by buying stores and eventually towers.  Each player picks a marker and starts at "receive $1500" (you do not collect the money until you have gone around the board once).  All players play around the outside edge (lower level) and can only move into the upper level (inside edge) with a bank payment of $15,000.  The players begin by rolling the dice, and the player with with the highest number starts first.  The rest of the order is determined by going clockwise.  When play begins, if a player rolls doubles, he/she is allowed to go again.  The player keeps rolling until he/she stops getting doubles (property that you hit along the way must be bid on or if it is owned the owner must be paid).  Each player is given $21,000 at the start of the game.  When a property is landed on, the bidding starts.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Colonnade is a two person game which uses a 1x8 board, 12 red checkers and 12 blue checkers.  The board is initially set up as shown in the figure.

You can pick up any one of your checkers from a stack, carrying the checkers above it along, and place it onto an adjacent stack that’s topped with a checker of your color. (A stack can be of height 1.)  Complete such a move with a discard.  I.e. if two like-colored checkers are adjacent in a stack, discard one of the two checkers.

You can also move a checker down to an unoccupied square.  You can't slide a bottom checker sideways to an unoccupied square.

The last player to have a move available wins.  I.e. if you don't have a move available on your turn, you lose.

In Wanderlust, you are racing through an arrow maze, trying to pick up the right Things and take those Things where you can win with them.

The playing area consists of a 6x7 grid of double-sided tiles. On one face, the tiles display the possible moves and on the other, places for winning and actions available to the players. After having done the action or move shown on a tile, the player flips the tile over.

Like Fluxx, the Goals are indefinite, but unlike Fluxx, Wanderlust requires much more turn-by-turn planning.

Complete rules, example turns, and PDFs are available at:
http://ceruleansgames.tripod.com/wanderlust.htm
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description: A children's game that is not actually about the wacky adventures of the eponymous seal mascot, but a way of teaching seamanship and environmental care among other things. Players navigate the archipelago on the game board with their boats and try to land on special tiles where they can gain point tokens. Depending on the colour of the tile, this is done by answering questions, drawing pictures or giving small performances. As an added twist, only some of the point tokens actually represent real points, meaning the scores of the players are hidden until the end of the game.

boardgame: Steel Will: 479 BC, The Battle of Plataea
description:
With a strong will like steel Spartans responsed to the call of war:
...
"Go tell the Spartans, stranger passing by,
that here, obedient to their law, we lie."
-Simonides.
...
"During the 2nd Persian Invasion to Greece  and after the battles of Thermopylae, Artemision and Boeotia led by Xerxes himself, the Persians retreated after their first loss at the naval Battle of Salamis. Persian forces retreated to Asia and General Mardonius returned to finish what the Persian King has started. He built a fortified camp outside Plataea.

Greeks have assembled an army allied by many cities of ancient Greece. Spartans led the forces of Peloponnesos , Athenians answered the call with many other cities. It was Summer of 479 BC where the two armies has met each other opposed , even if at the Percian forces had many greek traitors  and mercenaries,  for a battle that actually ended the Persian vanquishment.

General Mardonius met his own destiny in this battle, and General Pausanias from Sparta, has archived a victory , even if their forces were outnumbered."

"Steel Will: 479 BC, The Battle of Plataea" is a history-themed wargame for two players that simulates that battle. With an abstract taste of gameplay, the core of classic historic-wargame and the feeling of a new approach at combat mechanism, we tried to create something that keeps the interest of the players alive!"

A box set of three books containing additional rules and scenarios for first edition Warhammer:

Volume 1: Forces of Fantasy
Volume 2: Fighting Fantasy Battles
Volume 3: Arcane Magics

These include extended rules for fleshing out Warhammer armies, more battle rules (particularly for various levels of unit organization), and rules for the use of magic in the game.

From game designer:

This educational game, meant to help reinforce solving simple Algebraic equations, puts you in the roll of an explorer of a newly discovered land competing against other explorers in an effort to settle the most territories of the land.

The land consists of four different types of territories to settle.  The territories are the Plains, the Wetlands, the Forest, and the Mountains.

Each card represents a different territory and contains an equation to be solved.  In order to settle a territory one must possess a card containing the solution to the algebraic equation given on any unoccupied territory.  Forming empires can lead to overthrowing your opponent’s settlements.  But beware, if your opponent can fend off your attack, their settlements become even stronger than before.

The person who settles the most territories rules the land!

boardgame: Ravening Hordes: The Official Warhammer Battle Army Lists
description: A supplement for 2nd edition Warhammer that featured full army lists for that edition of the game - the immediate ancestor of the 3rd edition supplement, Warhammer Armies.  Included are rules for various armies along with points values, wargear, and full stats for the variety of units included with each.

A campaign book for the 2001 WFB campaign of the same name - includes rules for fighting in the campaign setting of Albion, including descriptions of the setting, special army lists for running battles, the running of the campaing itself, etc.

The Warhammer Fantasy Battle equivalent of Warhammer 40,000's Chapter Approved - a short-lived annual (the "Chronicles" name gets on the cover starting with the second annual - the first simply says "Annual"), collecting new and experimental rules from White Dwarf for Warhammer 6th edition.  These include rules for combat as well as new or revised army lists or special rules for various armies that didn't make it into the army books of the 6th edition.  Like Chapter Approved, each new edition was published in the year prior to the year mentioned in its title.

Are you tired of all the SPAM emails you get on a daily basis in your inbox? Well here is your chance to spam someone else for a change.

Junk Mail is card game all about spamming other people and trying to protect yourself. You will play spam cards on your opponents and give yourself firewalls, employees and professions for protection and better chances to spam someone else. Maybe you can use a hacker or an advertising rep. to help your cause.
family: Theme: Mail / Stamps / The Post Office

description:
Do you drive in traffic on a daily basis? How stressful is it dealing with all those crazy drivers every morning and night? Welcome to HONK! the board game all about driving through traffic.
The object of the game is to navigate your car down the freeway and be the first to make it home. That would be simple, but everyone else is trying to do the same thing. Each player has a handful of cards and a number of actions. A player may use cards to gain special movements, inhibit the movement of other players, or move cars out of way. The other thing players can do is strategically move their car. Either way, making it down the freeway is a hoot with flat tires, stalled cars, accidents, and of course the police. Keep your eyes open as this one will be for sale soon!

An optional rules supplement for Warhammer 6th edition with a focus on campaigns.  Included are rules for running several different kinds of campaigns (including map-based and decision trees), plus rules for special locations and environments and a number of scenarios to run during campaigns.

Learning game which can teach children to recognize sentence constituents. These are printed on little cards and can be combined in different ways. The goal is to form sentences to match the picture tiles included in the game.

EDB: Japanese Warfare was submitted for the "Dicefest Contest - A Tribute to Chessex" held in 2010.

It uses 36 dice in each players color to represent armies, units, actions, and rice production by each player.
Tokens are added to the game to enable hidden unit assignments to the armies, as well as an amount of castles and resources.

Set in feudal Japan, in the end of the Muromachi Period (1136 till 1568 A.D.) before firearms ever made it to the islands, the battles will be conducted between unit of archers, cavalry and Samurai.

EDB: Japanese Warfare is supposed to be the first of a series of games, which use a vast number of dice to represent units in battle scenarios and a simple action-point system to build and strengthen ones armies.

"An environmental sculpture in chrome steel and crystal clear polished playing boards which moves chess into an intriguing new dimension." (side of box Description)

This game is a relatively straightforward 3-Dimensional extrapolation of the standard chess concept, and as such the goal and theme are much the same, with one player each starting one the top and bottom boards.

Some of the moves for various pieces have been changed, with all pieces moving as per usual on one plane, but the movements from plane to plane of the Queen, Bishop, and Pawn are not as one would initially expect. Most other 3D moves are restricted to moving one square only directly above or below the initial position of the piece.

Board and set could also be used for several other Components: Three Dimensional Chess, as there are no original pieces.

boardgame: Bobby and Betty's Trip to the New York World's Fair
description: A souvenir of the 1939 world fair. The board reveals a miniature representation of the futuristic fair. So players could enjoy the various New York attractions in the comfort of their homes.

The thrill and action of real baseball! This tabletop baseball game has a "Snap-action" pitching mound that holds six balls and lets players control the speed of the pitch.
The "Snap-action" bat lets players control the power of the swing. Players hit the ball into the base hit pockets or go for a home run. The game includes scoring dials to record the score as the game progresses.

Changes your tabletop into a baseball stadium.

War has come to the ninth century as rival lords (the players) fight to conquer the land. Each player rules a fortress and the surrounding realm. Warriors are dispatched fortress and towers, crossing mountains, rivers, and open fields to engage the enemy. Using economics, diplomacy, and warfare, each player attempts to expand his realm and conquer all enemy fortresses.


     Players create a unique geography before each game begins, so it's never the same game twice.
     Antagonist is a BIG table game with marauding warriors, changing seasons, burning villages, and limitless strategies.
     Straight-forward, intuitive rules with have you playing in minutes.


(From the games label)

War has come to the ninth century as rival lords (the players) fight to conquer the land. Each player rules a fortress and the surrounding realm. Warriors are dispatched fortress and towers, crossing mountains, rivers, and open fields to engage the enemy. Using economics, diplomacy, and warfare, each player attempts to expand his realm and conquer all enemy fortresses.


     Players create a unique geography before each game begins, so it's never the same game twice.
     Battle Royale is a 2-player version of Antagonist with marauding warriors, changing seasons, burning villages, and limitless strategies.
     Straight-forward, intuitive rules with have you playing in minutes.


(From the games label)

In The Personality Game one player askes a multiple choice question to a random player. The player asking the question gets a point for correctly predicting what that player will say about themselves. The answering player's playing piece will point in a certain direction based on his answer. That direction leads to one of four different quadrants on a board representing a personality type.  At the end of the game, the place where a player's peice ends up indicates their personality.  There are also places on the board where players get stuck unless they can get other players to guess a word on a card from that personality type.  The stuck player lists examples of the word, without saying the word itself. If they match, they can move on.  Other players gain points for matching that word. The first player to score 6 points wins.

From the back of the box: How do you play Going Ape?  By roiling the dice and collecting fruits. Different for beginners and seasoned players, beginning players collect the same fruits but seasoned  players collect every fruit. The one who collects the most fruit wins and gets a monkey in reward. The one who has the most monkeys at the end gets the main prize.

Who will be first to Legoland? Go on an adventure through four different Lego Worlds as you make your way to Legoland! On every turn players use the remote control to complete one of six different types of activities on the DVD. You'll compete to win a Car Race Navigate a Maze, Shoot a Catapult, Spot the Difference, Play Hot Potato, or figure out the More or Less Lego's Challenge. Every time an activity is mastered the DVD instructs players how to move on the board. But watch out, it may tell players to move backward or switch places! The first player to reach Legoland wins! Game contains interactive DVD, game board, 6 Lego brick playing pieces, and instructions. For 2-6 players ages 6 and up.

--Back of Box

From the publisher:

"Space Frontier is a card game of space exploration. Can you colonize more planets with sufficient resources than the Invader fleet?

Choose from several different space ships to create your exploration fleet. Each ship has a different ability that is activated by discovering a planet with a specific resource type.

Once two systems have been fully explored (or the Invaders have explored one system), the game ends, and you add up the scores. If your point value is higher than the Invader's points, you win.

A variety of Invader ships let you adjust the difficulty of play, and advanced rules allow you to challenge yourself even further.

Space Frontier can be played solitaire, or with two players (head-to-head or cooperatively)."

Soldat is a set of rules for simulating squad level infantry combat during the early and mid Twentieth Century. The rules concentrate on the World Wars, but can also be used for Korea, Indochina and the early Arab-Israeli wars. 
Unlike the great majority of wargame rules for this period, Soldat concentrates on the role of the infantryman. Combat in both World Wars rapidly became a series of vicious, small unit actions, a classic confrontation at close quarters with no holds barred. As in most wars, the dirty business of winning battles fell to the infantryman. Despite the vast array of sophisticated and deadly weapons available to all sides, success or failure ultimately depended upon the ability of the foot soldier to take or hold ground.

From the publisher's website:

In the clever, creative, competitive, and captivating game of Kewbz™, opposing players compete for ownership of 'tower units' by positioning the final cube on top. Each time a corner is made, a cube is stacked at the intersection. When multiple corners are created, then multiple cubes are added into play. Clever strategy moves give you the winning edge.

A card game with gaming money. Bluff the value of the cards to gain a million.

The Players randomly draw cards containing different values. they then declare the value of the card, either the real value or bluffing. If the claim is not challenged by any other player, the active player received the amount of money he declared from the bank. 
If it is challenged (called) and the player was bluffing, he needs to pay the double amount declared to the calling player. If he was not bluffing the calling player needs to pay the double amount to the active player.
The player with most money when all the cards have been played wins.

From the box: Ready, set, ROLL! Land three or more marbles in a row and score!

Object: Be the first player to score 50 points. Score points by landing your marbles to create rows of three or more in the same quadrant.

User comment: The game consist of 25 marbles (5 each of 5 colors) and a circular board, about 12 inches in diameter. Around the outer rim of the board are 28  three-quarter inch holes. Between the holes and the rim of the board is where a player will roll his marble--in the style of a roulette wheel--and hope it lands in a desirable hole in a desirable quadrant of the board. Landing several marbles in a row gains points, whether they be three in a row of your color or of mixed colors.

Instructions are printed on the bottom of the box.

boardgame: The Trivia Game Of Ocala, Florida
description:
This is a generic trivia style game with an Ocala, Florida business theme. Ocala is the home of an internationally famous horse breeding and training center. The game features a colorful board with locations representing various business locations and tourist attractions.

Players move around the board and answer questions. The first player to get all the way through the grid to the jackpot square is the winner. The game also supports team play.

(c)1985 G & J Ideas

User SummaryThere are two Munchkin thumb drives:

     Munchkin Opposable Thumb Drive (fantasy theme)
     Munchkin Zombies Removable Thumb Drive (zombie theme)


				
				
					Munchkin Opposable Thumb DriveSince this thumb drive is "opposable," you can pull it out once per game during your Looking For Trouble phase and fight it like a monster. Its level is equal to the last two digits of the current year. If you defeat the Opposable Thumb Drive, you get 1 level and 1 Treasure. Its Bad Stuff is "Reformatted! You are dead."

				
				
					Munchkin Zombies Removable Thumb DriveOnce per game, you may remove a monster from combat and place the Monster card in your hand. It grants neither levels nor treasure. If this was the only monster in the fight, the combat ends immediately. The current player may Loot The Room, but may not Look For Trouble.

Rules from the World of Munchkin website. Both are 2GB in size and are fully functional flash drives.

Part of the Munchkin series.

boardgame: Murder Mystery Party: Death By Chocolate
description:
From the back of the box:

Everyone's a suspect when murder is on the menu. And with a Classic Detective Murder Mystery you have everything you need to serve up the perfect murder dinner party. First the invites, you choose which of your friends play which devious suspect and invite them to come along as that character. Then the shopping list, menu suggestions and recipes. Plus of course...a really tasty murder to solve. It will be drop dead fun, and by the end of the evening, one (or more) of the suspects, your 'friends', will be caught out as the murderer!

description:
From the German publisher website:
Die Thinx sind wahre Multitalente: Sie sprechen mehrere Sprachen, klingen mal wie das Rauschen eines Baches oder wie Verkehrslärm. Sie kennen verschiedene Tierarten und fordern unser Wissen heraus: Welche Thinx bilden die Paare? In welcher Reihenfolge müssen sie platziert werden? Und welche gehören zu einer Gruppe, welche nicht…?
Findet die richtigen Positionen der 8 intelligenten Thinx und enträtselt ihre geheimnisvollen Bande!

You know different animal species and a challenge to our knowledge: What are the Thinx couples? In what order they should be placed? And belonging to a group which does not ...?
Find the right positions of the 8 Thinx intelligent and deciphered their mysterious band!

Additional description:
"Thinx" is a thinking and logic game. The four basic pieces (from the main box) and the additional four pieces of the "Thinx" pack will be dressed in futuristic deco buildings. The goal of the game is to solve the puzzles by proper arrangement of the pieces on the eight fields of the board. The difficulty level rises from round to round. 
 In "Thinx" there are 3 different types of challenges:
- "Pair-search": Each 2 pieces fit together. Each must be positioned simultaneously on the board. The pairs are determined by the sound output of the Yvio. For example, a combination of "animal" / "loud noise". The stone with the sound output "elephant" should, therefore, a "Törööh" are assigned to stone - this is done through a trial and error "principle.
- "Order": In these challenges, the 8 stones are placed in an order (eg, single words to put together a lyrics or things will be sorted by size. Each stone solves it again exactly one sound).
- "Subset" Here, certain stones are sorted out following the instructions of the Yvio on the board.

Circulo is the world's first 4-in-1 circular strategy game; featuring four challenging games the whole family will enjoy, we're sure Circulo will become a favorite in your home for years to come!

4-in-a-Row: Players try to be the first to make a line of four tiles of their color in any direction to win!

Pipe Connections: The first player to complete a continuous pipe is the winner!

4 Letter Words: The first player to create a four letter word in any direction is the winner!

Sum of the Squares: The first player to line up tiles adding up to a total of seven is the winner!

Contents: 1 Circulo Game Tube with Cap, 1 Set of Pipe Connections Tiles, 1 Set of 4-in-a-Row Tiles, 1 Set of 4 Letter Words Tiles, 1 Set of Sum of the Squares Tiles

As day breaks upon Sekigahara pass, two armies stand ready to lay claim to the future of Japan. Two years before, Toyotomi Hideyoshi died leaving a son too young to rule. The council of regents sworn to defend his son split into two factions intent upon seizing power for themselves. As the fog lifts from the mountain pass, each faction stands poised for battle. Only the victor will witness the beginning of a new era in the history of Japan.

Akebono, the Japanese word meaning both “daybreak” and “beginning”, summarizes the tension and anticipation in the Sekigahara mountain pass, scene of the decisive battle of 1600 that would usher in the Edo period of Japanese history.

Akebono is a tactical dice driven game in which each player must present multiple flanks to ensure that at least some of the attacking troops will push forward. Units must be moved into a position to attack when their defeat is less likely.

In the end, the winner will be the player who best balances his aggressive and defensive tactics on the battlefield.

Pink Panther is a game for 2 to 4 players, in which one player pretends to be the Pink panther and the other players pretend to be either the Inspector, the Commissioner or the Constable. The Pink Panther is chased through the streets of Paris by the Inspector, the Commissioner and the Constable and has to reach the safety of one of his hideaways before he is caught

(from publisher's blurb)

Description from BoardgameNews.com:

The Caméléon deck includes 54 combination cards, each with a number, a color and an object, along with a single wild chameleon card. Each player starts the round by drawing one of these cards and laying it face up on the table.

A randomly chosen dealer then turns over cards from the deck one by one, and any player who wants the card on the top of the stack must cover it with his hand, then name the three elements on the card. If he's correct, he claims the card; if he collects three cards, each with one of the three elements on his target card, he wins the round. The first player to win two rounds wins the game.

This is the 2010 Promotion available for download off the Repos website for April Fool's.

Jean-Claude is aware. When he enters play, he is sideways, filling 2 spaces (his color is considered green).  If there aren’t 2 free adjacent spaces due to Buddha or Ghosts, Jean-Claude makes space next to a free space by discarding what is there (no curse/reward for ghost; can't eliminate an incarnation).

To defeat him, players must break both his legs: his left leg has a 3 Blue resistance and his right 4 Green (his right leg is stronger).
An exorcism must be successful on each of his two legs (the order has no importance). When one of his legs is broken, he moves fully on to the space for the remaining leg, leaving an open space.

When both of his legs are broken, he is discarded and gives an apple to the active player (this reward is an artifact).  An apple is good for the body and it’s full of pectin, but it can spoil!  During his Yang Phase, the player can discard the apple to return any Taoist (including himself) to 3 QI. But if another Wu-Feng comes into play before the apple is used, the apple has spoiled and it must be discarded with no effect.
The apple cannot be used on a dead Taoist.

http://rprod.com/uploads/file/GHOST_STORIES_GOODIE_US_JCVD.pdf
